data.title: 5 Themes Shaping Global Real Estate In 2026: European Investors' Perspectives
data.description:
By Rich Hill, Senior Managing Director - Global Head of Real Estate Research and Strategy

A market defined by complexity and opportunity

Investor conversations across Europe (and globally) during the first quarter consistently begin with a discussion around the global macro backdrop. The environment remains unusually complex, as geopolitical tensions are elevated, policy outcomes are uncertain, and interest rate expectations continue to shift. Yet, despite this noise, real estate investors are increasingly focused on what is within their control: driving net operating income (NOI) growth through selective property, market, and manager execution.

Broadly, a consensus has emerged that the real estate cycle has entered a recovery phase. What differs is the shape of that recovery. Rather than a traditional U- or V-shaped rebound, a more accurate characterization is one of a K-shaped recovery, marked by widening return dispersion across sectors, geographies, and strategies. This somewhat differentiated perspective, coupled with renewed attention on public REIT market signals, ongoing resilience in lease-driven cash flows, and growing conviction in sectors including data centers, multifamily, and logistics, formed the foundation of many investor discussions.

The perspectives below outline the top five themes for European investors today and highlight the factors that may prove most consequential in navigating the next stage of the cycle.

1. Geopolitical risks: Elevated but not derailing CRE fundamentals

Tensions in the Middle East remain top of mind for European investors. Despite these risks, the overall assessment is that commercial real estate remains on a stable footing, and notably, our 2026 outlook has not materially changed. Long term lease structures continue to support income stability, and fundamentals—outside of office and select life sciences segments—are proving resilient.

Investor sentiment has gradually improved, supported by early signs of recovery in capital markets activity. The ultimate economic impact will depend on the duration and intensity of geopolitical disruptions, particularly as they relate to energy markets and global trade. In this environment, investors are best served by focusing on what they can control: driving net operating income growth.

Our macro strategy colleagues have addressed this in recent reports: Market Implications Amid Renewed Geopolitical Uncertainty and U.S. & Israel vs. Iran: A Sharpening Geopolitical Fault Line.

2. Interest rates: Stability matters more than direction

A key theme across recent conversations was the movement in the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield, which has risen from 3.94% to 4.17% as of March 9. While geopolitical tensions can lift long-term yields, the term premium has been relatively stable, suggesting the recent increase may reflect rising inflation expectations, including the impact of oil surpassing $100 per barrel. (As measured by the Adrian, Crump & Moench 10-Year Treasury Term Premium Model, it has remained relatively stable at around +64 bps.)

Commercial real estate generally provides a hedge against most inflationary environments, with the notable exception of stagflation, when interest rates rise due to higher inflation while economic growth stagnates. That scenario represents the clearest downside risk. At the same time, a sharp decline in 10-year Treasury yields may not deliver the relief rally some expect, as it could signal a weaker macro backdrop characterized by slower growth and widening credit spreads.

The most favorable environment for real estate would be rate stability rather than dramatic moves in either direction. Since real rates matter more than nominal rates, given the strong historical relationship between inflation and NOI growth, a 10-year Treasury anchored around 4.0%–4.25%, paired with inflation near 3%, represents a historically constructive backdrop for CRE. Under this scenario, real rates would be roughly 1% (4% nominal minus 3% inflation). For context, real rates have averaged about 1.49% since 1997, 1.7% from 1997 to 2019, and 2.9% from 1997 to 2007 - periods during which CRE delivered attractive returns.

3. Economic conditions: Resilience on the surface, divergence beneath

Tariff concerns have been noticeably less prominent this year. Instead, investors focused on the underlying health of the U.S. consumer and the widening bifurcation within the economy. Importantly, while U.S. GDP growth finished 2025 at 2.2% and consensus expects +2.5% in 2026, headline resilience conceals a K-shaped economy. Consider:

  • Higher income households continue to drive consumption.
  • Lower income cohorts face deteriorating wage growth and rising delinquencies on student loans, credit cards, and other borrowing.
  • As such, expect that affordability (or lack thereof) will likely be a significant focus of U.S. midterm elections in November.

In Europe, 2025 GDP growth exceeded expectations and was upgraded to 1.4% by year‑end, supported by fiscal stimulus, delayed tariff impacts, and broadly prudent policy decisions. While the Eurozone was not the world’s top‑performing region, its resilience highlighted meaningful improvements in competitiveness, productivity, and overall economic discipline. Despite lingering skepticism following years of underperformance, global investors are increasingly recognizing that the region’s structural fundamentals have strengthened. As a result, cautious optimism is building that the Eurozone may be moving toward a more equal footing with the United States, helped in part by ongoing stimulus measures. Interestingly, U.S. investors appear somewhat more optimistic on Eurozone growth prospects than European investors themselves, who remain wary after several years of modest outcomes.

4. The CRE recovery: Broadly underway, sharply diverging

It’s a consensus view that the global real estate cycle has moved into recovery: U.S. ODCE indices have risen for 6 consecutive quarters, and Europe for 7 quarters. Europe’s trailing four-quarter unlevered returns (~6.8%) currently outpace the U.S. (~5%), consistent with our view that Europe would likely outperform the U.S. despite lower cap rates, given improving economic conditions and lower sovereign debt yields, which make leverage more accretive. However, headline returns mask meaningful dispersion:

  • In Europe, the spread between top quartile and bottom quartile ODCE funds is more than 300 bps.
  • In the U.S., the gap is even wider, with bottom quartile funds reporting slightly negative returns.
  • In other words, the top U.S. funds are outperforming in line with Europe, but the bottom U.S. funds are underperforming their European peers.

Furthermore, single-sector strategies in Europe have materially outperformed multi-sector portfolios, reinforcing the idea that selectivity, not broad-market beta, will drive returns in this phase of the cycle. This aligns with our view that the recovery is K-shaped, and with limited expectations for cap-rate compression, NOI growth becomes the primary driver of both income and capital appreciation.

5. Sector themes: Where are investors leaning in?

Public markets have historically been an accurate leading indicator of private real estate in both downturns and recoveries, but they can also serve as an important barometer of secular shifts. With that in mind, investors were interested in digging into the meaningful absolute and relative outperformance of U.S.-listed REITs in the first two months of the year (+10.5%) and in February (+7.5%) when AI concerns intensified. Data centers, logistics, and housing continue to resonate with investors across both the U.S. and Europe. To keep the discussion focused and concise, conversations on opportunities in Europe centered on the following:

Data centers: Structural tailwinds and demand outpacing supply

Data centers remain Europe’s strongest property type, with demand expected to outpace supply for the fourth consecutive year. Revenue per available facility (RevPAF) is projected to grow at a 6.5% CAGR from 2025 to 2029, underscoring the depth and durability of tenant requirements. The five largest European markets (Frankfurt, London, Paris, Amsterdam, and Dublin) are set to capture roughly 48% of 2025 take-up, and vacancy in these core hubs continues to tighten, now at 6.9% overall (with Dublin at 4.4% and Frankfurt at 4.6%).

At the same time, differentiation within the sector is increasing. The most compelling opportunities lie in cloud-oriented data centers (both hyperscale and colocation) located in Availability Zone (AZ) clusters across FLAPD markets, as well as select emerging locations such as Madrid and Milan. These assets benefit from entrenched tenant demand, established fiber connectivity, and strong utilization, resulting in more predictable cash flows and lower operational, security, and AI-related risk exposure.

Demand is being shaped not only by AI adoption but also by regulatory preferences. Many European corporates and governments prefer data to reside within European jurisdictions. A recent BARC study found that 84% of surveyed companies view data sovereignty as central to their strategy, with 70% reporting a sharply higher level of relevance over the past one to two years. This push further strengthens demand for cloud infrastructure within Europe rather than diverting workloads to larger scale U.S. markets.

Availability Zones themselves provide a strategic advantage: as clusters of interconnected facilities with high-speed, low-latency fiber, they enable communication in milliseconds and offer tenants resilience, redundancy, and performance reliability. High barriers to entry, including power constraints, land scarcity, and long permitting timelines, help maintain the current favorable supply-demand imbalance.

Logistics: Normalization is concealing widening performance divergence

Europe’s industrial and logistics market has largely normalized. Vacancy, take-up, and rental growth have softened, and performance has converged with that of traditional sectors. Rents rose 3.3% in Q3 2025 versus 11.7% in Q3 2022.

Beneath this surface normalization, the sector’s outlook is becoming more selective. Occupiers are increasingly prioritizing modern, efficient, future-proof assets. A widening quality premium is emerging, driven by three forces:

  1. Rapid technological advancement in AI-enabled warehousing, robotics, and fulfillment.
  2. Tightening environmental and sustainability standards.
  3. A limited new supply is expected to enter the market in 2026.

These dynamics are most apparent in the Netherlands, where fundamentals remain tight and demand is reinforced by the country’s role as Europe’s primary gateway through Rotterdam. By contrast, the UK has softened, with rising vacancy and moderating rental growth after several strong years. That shift is creating opportunities to develop or redevelop modern logistics properties where existing stock falls short of occupier requirements.

European residential: Deep structural support, strong returns, and local nuance

European residential remains resilient, anchored by longstanding structural undersupply. Single sector European residential funds delivered +7.3% net returns in 2025, the strongest across major property types. Demand should hold through 2026 and beyond, supported by migration, shifting household formation, urbanization, and longer rental tenures as affordability delays homeownership in major cities. Institutional investment is set to deepen, with Europe still roughly 15 years behind the U.S. in market penetration.

Performance will vary by market. The UK benefits from favorable demographics, no rent caps, and a gradual move toward institutional ownership. Spain continues to see strong price growth fueled by demand, migration, and constrained supply. The Netherlands remains highly constrained but continues to attract institutional capital due to its stable fundamentals.

Despite these favorable tailwinds, there is growing recognition that the challenge is not simply undersupply but a mismatch between the types and locations of housing and where demand exists. This misalignment will increasingly shape strategy and outcomes across the sector.

The bottom line for investors

Despite a noisy macro backdrop, conversations across Europe pointed in the same direction: the real estate recovery is underway but uneven. Investor attention is squarely on controllable drivers, like growing NOI through selective assets, markets, and managers, while rate stability, rather than large moves, is viewed as the most supportive backdrop. Client interest broadly aligns with our 2026 Outlook: data centers, residential, and modern logistics—where demand is durable and supply remains tight—stand out as the most resilient opportunities.

Bottom line: allocate to sectors with structural demand and constrained supply, focus on NOI growth, and prioritize managers with a track record of disciplined execution. The recovery ahead will be driven by execution, not macro headlines.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Qualcomm: New Revenue Will Stem The Pain Of Losing Apple
data.description:
Qualcomm (QCOM) has become a household name in the smartphone industry, providing chips across the iOS / Android divide for more than a decade and presiding over some spectacular advances in processing power, efficiency, and wireless connectivity.

But with Apple (AAPL) preparing to replace their wireless chips with in-house designs, Qualcomm will need to become more than a smartphone supplier and leverage their technical innovations in new industries to increase revenue and maintain the valuation multiples they enjoy today.

Qualcomm is not blind to this make-or-break transition and has several opportunities in automotive, IoT, and Windows laptops to grow the business. Below I’ll model whether those revenue streams can buoy Qualcomm’s falling share price before Apple takes off, or if there’s more downside before Qualcomm can turn things around.

How quickly can Qualcomm diversify away from smartphone revenue?

Qualcomm will rely on growing revenue from its Automotive and IoT segments to make up for slowing growth in its smartphone SOC segment, where some 80% of global Android shipments include Qualcomm components. QCOM will likely retain this dominance in Android designs that will power mid-single digit revenue growth, but losing Apple market share to their own custom wireless chips will reduce revenue by up to 20% that no amount of incremental Android sales will be able to replace.

By 2030, Qualcomm forecasts that $8 billion in sales from the automotive segment (the Snapdragon Digital Chassis) will be one of the biggest revenue drivers for the business. That includes technology like Digital Cockpit (powering in-car interfaces like instrument clusters, infotainment systems, and voice assistants), Autonomous Driver Assist Systems (ADAS) that process data from cameras and sensors and generate AI self-driving outputs, and vehicle networking and services capabilities like WiFi and Bluetooth connections and OTA updates.

A recent McKinsey report projects a CAGR of around 19% for the automotive market from 2025 to 2035. Competitors here have strong existing relationships but generally inferior products, with Qualcomm still likely to capture the majority of business through industry-leading power, efficiency, and hardware integration. Toyota’s RAV 4 is one high profile example of Qualcomm auto penetration, and Stellantis uses Qualcomm solutions across its global brand lineup. Existing partnerships to enable ADAS features in Mercedes-Benz (BENZ:CA) and BMW (BMWKY) vehicles indicate that global automotive OEMs are choosing Qualcomm hardware for their next generation capabilities, and a pipeline of more than $45 billion indicates healthy demand for Qualcomm’s automotive solutions.

Snapdragon PCs, Mixed Reality, and Industrial IoT

In 2024, Qualcomm released the Snapdragon X Series chipset designed for the PC market and specifically for on-device AI use cases. These chips are less expensive and more efficient than their counterparts from Intel and AMD while delivering comparable performance. Adoption has been held back by incompatibility with applications designed to run on x86 architecture (with emulation providing a semi-viable workaround early on), but this has mostly been overcome through an integration push from Microsoft and other partners.

Lower SOC unit costs may drive renewed interest in Qualcomm chips, as PC manufacturers push to compete with Apple’s new MacBook Neo that has received rave reviews and in early analysis, appears to be an existential threat to the budget PC market share. While it has seen only modest interest so far (limited mostly to Copilot+ use cases on Snapdragon PCs), industry-leading onboard AI performance may also become a stronger selling point over time, as LMM models are miniaturized and privacy concerns push more compute onto local devices.

Mixed Reality (XR)

Most thoughts of virtual, alternative, augmented, and general “mixed” reality hardware conjure images of Apple’s Vision Pro or Meta’s (META) Quest headsets. Outside of some enterprise and hardcore gaming use cases, prospects for this style of hardware are dubious and will drive little incremental revenue in my view. But these early iterations of XR hardware are dominated by Qualcomm silicon, with the company established as the definitive leader in XR hardware, powering devices with 90% share of the market by 2024 with solutions like the XR2 Gen 2 platform (in devices like Meta Quest 3 with 75% market share) and the more powerful XR2+ Gen 2 platform (powering upcoming devices like the Samsung Galaxy XR and Sony Spatial HMD).

Much more promising are products like Meta’s Ray-Ban smart glasses (the latest version powered by Qualcomm's AR1 Gen 1 platform) that, combined with AI, provide a much less intrusive way to interact with existing social platforms and record and share content while remaining relatively stylish and present.

I think (and wrote about) Apple’s upcoming smart glasses product will bring ecosystem integration and practical functionality to new heights, stimulating significant new demand for the form factor and essentially “creating” a new market segment (at scale) similar to the release of the iPad in 2010. If so, Qualcomm is the easy favorite to power the vast majority of non-Apple XR devices.

Industrial IoT

Qualcomm’s industrial IoT ambitions span a range of industries and use cases, but generally leverage strengths in providing a strong performance / power efficiency ratio and industry-leading wireless connectivity.

The Qualcomm IQ Series is designed to provide efficient compute, allowing AI applications to perform “at the edge,” or directly on-site. This has applications in on-site video threat monitoring, where an edge SOC can analyze dangerous objects or behavior and raise an alert, or in optimizing robot / drone pathing in automated warehouses, both use cases where latency and local EM interference make cloud processing impractical.

The Qualcomm Aware platform also covers the other end of the spectrum, where data is aggregated in the cloud and unlocks large scale insights into supply chain logistics.

Overall, the industrial IoT segment will probably require significant solutions engineering in the early phases, but with the potential to scale into integral components of customers’ supply chains that drive ongoing revenue. Qualcomm does not break out specific industrial IoT design wins or pipeline dollar or deal volume, but management indicated on the most recent earnings call that combined Auto and IoT growth are outpacing run rates required to achieve 2029 revenue targets of $8 billion and $4 billion (for specifically Industrial IoT), respectively, which are used to derive segment growth forecasts in the valuation below.

Valuation

In the Financial Update from Qualcomm’s 2024 Investor Day, the company targets a 50/50 split for revenue from smartphones and Auto and IoT combined, which represent about 25% of the business in Q1 2026. To hit annual revenue targets of $8 billion in Auto and $14 billion in IoT (including PC, XR, Industrial, and smaller markets like networking and smartwatches), they will need to have a CAGR of 16% and 20%, respectively, over the next four years.

I model a worst-case scenario where Apple removes Qualcomm chips from all its products beginning in 2027, cutting 20% of handset revenue that has no obvious replacement.

Comps

Broadcom (AVGO) stands out in this comparison as a major beneficiary of the AI boom, pushing P/E and revenue per employee well above sector medians. But Qualcomm’s strong asset turnover generates industry-leading return on total capital, in part because it has not been positioned to be flooded with investment from the first wave of hyperscaler AI spending.

NXP (NXPI) and Skyworks (SWKS) represent customers/competitors in the automotive and wireless connectivity markets with P/E and P/B ratios that would unfairly discount Qualcomm's other business lines, including Snapdragon PCs and XR, but could provide insight into how sentiment could push down multiples when the other shoe finally drops with Apple.

Risks

Qualcomm has a strong technical edge and a good path forward, but there will also be many opportunities to get off track.

  • The automotive pipeline looks good, but continued customer acquisition over the next half decade will be essential to building this into a leading revenue segment for the business.

  • While most compatibility issues have likely been solved for Snapdragon PCs, this has been a historically competitive segment with incumbents that will not go down without a fight. Qualcomm will need to continue to push the performance/efficiency envelope to attain the 50%+ share targeted for 2029.

  • Industrial IoT is growing rapidly, but here too incumbents exist with much greater domain experience and established relationships. This could be the biggest risk to Qualcomm’s long-term vision if solutions engineering or large-scale adoption of IoT wins end up taking longer than anticipated.

Conclusion

Qualcomm faces something of a Sword of Damocles situation with revenue from Apple, a prize that has been slipping away for years that will inevitably reduce revenue and could cause some to rerate valuation multiples for a business that has traditionally relied on smartphone integrations for a majority of its income.

But once we move past that difficult time (which could present a buying opportunity for patient investors), I think Qualcomm will be in a very strong position to make hay from some of the second-order impacts of AI adoption, as software-based vehicles ramp up their market share and both consumers and businesses explore and realize the benefits of on-device (or “edge”) computing for privacy-preserving and low-latency use cases.

I rate Qualcomm a Hold until the Apple revenue hit materializes, when we’ll check in on progress in the automotive and IoT segments and see how much sentiment is really impacted. I project a price target of $200 by 2030, growth of 43% from prices today, if Qualcomm can maintain their technical edge and diversify into the business they set out to be.

data.title: Whale's Insight: Crypto's TradFi Pipes Are Expanding And Oil Perps Are Booming
data.description:
This week, crypto’s integration with TradFi accelerated as Kraken secured a Fed master account and Morgan Stanley moved its spot crypto ETF filings forward. Meanwhile, crude-driven macro volatility triggered a sharp surge in TradFi-linked perp activity, with oil perps trading above $2B per day. Beneath the turbulence, persistent spot ETF inflows into XRP and SOL point to selective demand and likely institutional positioning in major altcoin leaders.

Crypto’s Convergence with TradFi Accelerates

Kraken Becomes First Crypto Bank With Direct Fed Access

Kraken Financial received approval from the Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City on March 4, 2026, for a limited-purpose master account, becoming the first digital asset bank in U.S. history to gain direct access to the Federal Reserve's core payment infrastructure.

  • This historic milestone breaks the longstanding barrier preventing crypto-native firms from direct participation in the U.S. central bank's payment systems, integrating crypto deeper into mainstream finance.

  • Kraken can now settle USD transactions directly via Fedwire, enabling faster, cheaper, and more reliable fiat movements for institutional clients while eliminating reliance on intermediary banks and associated de-banking risks.

  • It significantly enhances Kraken's institutional offerings (e.g., OTC, custody, staking), boosts operational efficiency, and strengthens its positioning ahead of a potential IPO.

  • The approval sets a regulatory precedent, providing a clear pathway for other compliant crypto firms (such as Coinbase or Crypto.com) to pursue similar access and potentially sparking a wave of applications.

  • Overall, it elevates the legitimacy and institutional trust of the crypto industry, accelerating its convergence with traditional financial rails and supporting broader adoption of assets like BTC and ETH.

Morgan Stanley Advances Spot ETF Filings

Morgan Stanley Investment Management submitted Amendment No. 1 (S-1/A) to the SEC on March 4, 2026, for its spot Bitcoin and spot Solana ETFs, advancing the applications from preliminary intent to a more concrete and actionable stage. This marks the first such move by a major U.S. bank and one of the most significant institutional developments in crypto for 2026.

  • With over $4 trillion in assets under management, Morgan Stanley’s vast wealth management and institutional client network could channel substantial capital into these ETFs once approved, especially from conservative institutions and high-net-worth individuals who have hesitated to hold crypto directly, significantly boosting institutional adoption and liquidity for Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and Solana (SOL-USD).

  • Major institutional entries have historically triggered strong price rallies; Morgan Stanley’s involvement signals Wall Street’s full pivot to crypto and is likely to spark renewed FOMO and upward momentum for BTC and SOL.

  • The new products will heighten competition among existing spot BTC ETFs that have already gathered hundreds of billions in assets under management while also expanding the overall market size and improving investor choice.

  • In the long run, it solidifies crypto as a legitimate asset class, encouraging other major banks and traditional institutions to follow suit and accelerating the transition of Bitcoin and Solana to mainstream financial instruments.

BlackRock Private Credit Stress From TradFi Liquidity to DeFi

BlackRock's $26 billion private credit fund has begun restricting investor redemptions in response to surging withdrawal requests, signaling a fresh crack in the $3.5 trillion private credit market following similar pressures at Blue Owl and potentially triggering ripple effects on crypto prices and DeFi markets through macro contagion and tokenized RWAs.

If outflows force asset sales and deleveraging in private credit, risk appetite could weaken across markets, indirectly weighing on risk assets, including Bitcoin.

A more direct spillover runs through RWAs: tokenized private credit can bring traditional credit stress on-chain, where defaults or bankruptcies quickly erode collateral health and trigger liquidations and tighter liquidity.

The First Brands bankruptcy, for example, drove volatility in positions linked to Midas RWA and pushed protocols such as Morpho close to liquidation, highlighting the fragility created by deeper DeFi–TradFi credit integration.

March 18 FOMC Preview

Ahead of the March 18 FOMC decision, market-based forecasts are firmly anchored to a hold, with roughly a 99% probability assigned to no change.

The near-term constraint is energy-driven inflation. Higher oil prices complicate the disinflation path and reduce the Fed’s room to cut.

The counterweight is a cooling labor backdrop, with revisions painting a weaker trend than the initial prints suggest.

Over the past 10 months, 8 labor releases were later revised down, often by a meaningful margin. In one notable instance, an initial +147K gain was revised to -13K, a swing of roughly 160K jobs. The latest reading was -92K versus an expectation of +58K, a miss of about 150K, reinforcing the view that underlying labor momentum has softened.

Another potential volatility catalyst is the anticipated May leadership transition to Chair Warsh, which could shift policy communication and reprice the expected path of cuts.

Market pricing also shows active debate around whether cuts arrive by June, with expectations drifting later and the earliest plausible first cut increasingly clustering in the summer.

Crude Volatility Sparks a Perps Volume Surge

Middle East headlines triggered a sharp oil repricing, with WTI futures briefly surging more than 30% toward the $120 area. With major TradFi venues closed over the weekend, on-chain TradFi perpetuals became a key 24/7 outlet for hedging and expressing the shock in real time.

Crude-related perp turnover accelerated from below $100M/day at the end of February to above $2B/day recently. On Hyperliquid, crude-linked perps printed over $1.2B in 24-hour volume, ranking just behind BTC perps at ~$3.5B and ahead of ETH perps at ~$900M over the same window.

Broader perp activity also rebounded, with total perp DEX volume above $10B and traditional assets such as crude, gold, and U.S. equities representing more than 30% of that total.

The structural takeaway is that on-chain perps are increasingly becoming the go-to venue for 24/7 macro hedging, reinforced by permissionless listings that allow commodities, metals, and index-linked contracts to be deployed quickly when headlines hit.

Altcoin Leader Inflows Signal Resilience

Despite elevated uncertainty weighing on crypto markets, altcoins are showing early signs of resilience.

Total3, which tracks the market cap of altcoins excluding Ethereum (ETH-USD), has been consolidating in a $640B to $740B range and is up about 11% since early February, suggesting capital remains selectively deployed in altcoins.

XRP (XRP-USD) is showing constructive signals. Binance (BNB-USD) recorded several sharp spikes in XRP withdrawal transactions in recent days, including more than 14,000 withdrawals on March 6, consistent with accumulation followed by transfers to private wallets.

Separately, demand for XRP ETFs continues to firm. Cumulative inflows have surpassed $1.4B, and monthly ETF flows have remained net positive since November 2025.

Solana shows a similar flow profile. Since the spot SOL ETF launched in October 2025, monthly spot netflows have stayed positive, even as SOL is down close to 60% over the same period.

Overall, persistent spot ETF inflows into XRP and SOL through volatility likely reflect ongoing institutional positioning and highlight durable demand for major altcoins despite constrained liquidity across the broader altcoin market.

Week Ahead

  • Mar 14: US Initial Jobless Claims Release

  • Mar 18: Federal Reserve FOMC Interest Rate Decision

  • Mar 19: ECB and Bank of Japan Interest Rate Decision

  • Mar 17: Producer Price Index for February 2026

Next week’s focus shifts to major central bank rate decisions amid heightened oil-driven volatility. The base case remains a Fed hold, while the recent inflation impulse from energy likely raises the bar for near-term easing across the Fed, ECB, and Bank of Japan, cooling rate-cut expectations at the margin.

In the U.S., jobless claims will be a key cross-check: if labor conditions stabilize or improve while inflation expectations re-accelerate, markets may push the expected timing of the first cut further out.

data.title: B2Gold: A Weaker 2026 Setting Up A Stronger Future
data.description:
Introduction

B2Gold (BTG) is a Canadian gold producer with operations across four mines located in Mali, Philippines, Namibia, and Canada. B2Gold has faced a turbulent period with security concerns in Mali and rising development costs at its Goose Mine in Canada putting the shares under pressure.

These issues have now come to pass. Operations in Mali continue uninterrupted, with agreements reached under the country's new mining code, and the Goose mine is in production and currently ramping up. Supported by the higher gold price, the shares rose 89% in 2025.

Despite this strong return, the shares still lagged the wider VanEck Gold Miners Index (GDX) which rose 155% in 2025. This underperformance reflects continued investor concerns around Mali and weak execution at the company.

2026 is set to remain a transition year. Cost guidance on the surface appeared weak, but dig in closer and it's all about setting up for a stronger 2027 and reflective of higher royalties from the current gold price. With the gold prepay agreement expiring in June 2026 this offers another boost. This allows 66,000 ounces of quarterly production to be sold at market prices, currently ~$5,100/oz, compared to the $2,191/oz in the prepay, suggesting a significant uplift to revenue could be coming.

With this in mind, let's explore B2Gold's latest earnings, cover the guidance for 2026, and ultimately explain why I believe B2Gold shares still represent good value.

Q4 Earnings

B2Gold recently reported their Q4 '25 earnings, reporting quarterly production of 303,029 oz of gold. This marked an increase of ~117,000 oz (+63%) from the previous year. This increase was driven by the Goose Mine in Canada, which entered production earlier in 2025 and produced 38,616 oz in Q4.

Fekola, located in Mali, had a strong end to the year; production rose 95% from a year earlier to reach 163,720 oz. This increase largely came from improved grades (2.29 g/t vs. 1.17 g/t), and a small increase in recovery rates (92.4% vs. 91.9%). This came as a higher-grade zone of the mine was reached, and gold production from the 2025 permitted Fekola underground began.

Masbate in the Philippines, continued its strong production record, producing 49,900 oz in Q4. Whilst this only represents a ~1% increase from 2024 levels, this is better than initially expected due to higher mill throughput and higher recoveries (78% vs. 74.1%), offset by a slight fall in grades (0.91 g/t vs. 0.95 g/t).

B2Gold's final mine, Otjikoto in Namibia, also had a strong performance. Despite open-pit mining having ended and the mine processing open-pit stockpiles, production fell only slightly compared to 2024 coming in at 50,793 oz (-3%). Falling grades (1.91 g/t vs. 2.10 g/t) were offset by a 6% rise in tonnes milled, with recoveries remaining stable (98.7%). Ore processing from stockpiles is set to continue at the mine until the Antelope deposit is fully developed, expected to begin production in late 2027.

For the full year, production came in at 979,604 oz, within B2Gold's guidance range of 970,000 and 1,075,000 ounces.

B2Gold achieved an average realized gold price of $3,718 per share in the quarter, 40% higher than Q4 ‘24, as gold continued its upward rally. For many, this gold price may seem low, especially considering all the headlines about gold at over $5,000 an ounce. The reason for this is a gold-prepay agreement that requires delivery of 66,000 ounces a quarter at a price of $2,191. This is set to run until June 2026 and represents the delivery of 264,775 ounces, with the revenue being realized upon the gold's delivery.

283,490 ounces of gold were sold during the quarter, 20 Koz lower than production, attributed to shipment timings from Fekola. Accounting for this, revenue came in at $1.05 billion, 111% higher than in Q4 '24.

Looking at cash flow, B2Gold reported cash flow from operations of $290.6 million, a significant increase from the $120.5 million reported a year earlier, as the company benefited from the higher gold price and increased production.

On the balance sheet side, B2Gold ended the quarter with $380.4 million in cash and a total debt of $598 million. With cash requirements for capex greatly reduced following Goose in production and the current cash flow from gold at over $5,000/oz, I believe the current balance sheet looks strong, with no major solvency risks. Once the gold prepay agreement ends in June, B2Gold should experience a substantial cash flow acceleration, strengthening the balance sheet.

Also on the balance sheet is a portfolio of investments valued at ~$600 million. Like B2Gold, these investments have been performing strongly due to the rising gold price, alongside company-specific factors. This also provides B2Gold with some optionality by providing potential future growth opportunities.

On the costs front, B2Gold reported all-in sustaining costs [AISC] coming in at $1,754 per ounce, a $106/oz increase from a year earlier. The biggest contributor to this rise was the Goose Mine, which recorded an AISC of $2,249/oz, the highest of B2Gold's mines. With commercial production only declared in Q4 last year, costs should fall as production ramps up and capital expenditures to increase crushing capacity are complete. Goose will have a higher cost in 2026, before a sharp fall in AISC is expected in 2027.

At Fekola, AISC was $1,903/oz (-15%). This was higher than expected but is down to the higher gold price resulting in increased royalty costs, and the sales timing of 20,500 oz of gold being pushed into Q1.

Masbate AISC was $1,230/oz (+12%) during the quarter as increasing royalties increased costs. An increase in AISC was also observed at Otjikoto, with a rise to $1,085/oz (+19%) as increased capital expenditures on the Wolfshag underground mine development and increased royalties pushed up costs.

Overall, this translated to a net profit of $180.3 million, an improvement from the net loss of $9 million a year earlier. On an adjusted basis, earnings also increased, coming in at $147.3 million, up from $17.4 million a year earlier, and equivalent to $0.11 per share, a $0.07 miss from expectations.

For B2Gold, this quarter marks the continued growth of the company, not just from higher gold prices, but from increased production. With the Goose mine ramping up and hitting 2025 production guidance, B2Gold is setting itself up for a positive future.

Recent Developments

2026 Guidance: Weak on the Surface, Strategic Beneath

Alongside their results, B2Gold released their guidance for 2026. On the surface, guidance appears weak with lower production and a sharp increase in costs. However, the figures reflect a transition year as B2Gold undertakes several major investments and continues ramping up the Goose mine.

For 2026, B2Gold guides for production of 820,000-970,000 ounces of gold, below the 979,604 ounces produced in 2025. Production is set to be driven higher as the Goose mine continues its ramp-up, with the midpoint of production guidance at 200,000 oz.

All other mines are expected to see falls in production; Otjikoto is guided at 80,000 oz (2025: 199,139), a sharp fall from 2025 following the end of open-pit mining activities; Masbate is guided at 180,000 oz (2025: 196,526 oz), a small fall from 2025 largely due to a lower expected recovery rate; and Fekola is guided at 435,000 oz (2025: 530,739) due to the timing of mine stripping.

Costs are where the headline numbers appear concerning. B2Gold guides to cash operating costs of $1,155–$1,280/oz and AISC of $2,400–$2,580/oz, significantly higher than the $1,584/oz achieved in 2025.

This, though, is largely due to transitional factors. Deferred stripping at the Fekola complex in Mali is raising near-term costs but should unlock higher-grade ore in future years. Second, Goose is only partially ramped up, meaning fixed costs are spread over fewer ounces, and significant capital expenditure is still required to upgrade the mill. At Otjikoto, open-pit mining has ended, and it's now primarily processing lower-grade stockpiles. Otjikoto's production should rise and AISC fall once development of the Antelope deposit is complete, with this deposit set to see average production of 110,000 oz annually between 2029 and 2032 at an AISC of only $1,095/oz.

Finally, the gold price itself is pushing up costs. B2Gold uses a gold price assumption of $5,000/oz, resulting in significant royalty costs: $525/oz overall, rising to $910/oz at Fekola. For every $100 fall in the gold price, AISC is expected to fall $12.

Whilst AISC may be high this year, at current gold prices this still results in high operating margins and strong profitability. More importantly, this is likely the peak of AISC for the next few years. In 2027, higher grade ore will see Fekola production rise and reduced stripping required, a new crusher at Goose will reduce required capex spending and see costs spread over higher production, and high-grade ore from the Antelope deposit at Otjikoto will lead to a lower AISC.

The guidance is quite clear: B2Gold is facing a higher cost year of elevated costs and uneven production to set it up for a stronger future. Instead of being in different years, these higher costs are concentrated in 2026. If all proceeds as planned, B2Gold should exit 2026 with increased production, positioning it for a much stronger 2027 where AISC should fall.

A New CEO

CEO Clive Johnson is retiring and set to be replaced by current CFO Michael Cinnamond.

While the stock has seen a strong performance under his leadership, rising 89% in 2025, it has undoubtedly underperformed peers, with the wider VanEck Gold Miners Index up 155% in the same period.

The CEO cannot be entirely blamed for this underperformance; cost overruns at Goose were driven by a high inflation period necessitating a gold prepay just before gold prices soared. Significant uncertainty in Mali, with a new mining code and the arrest of other miners executives, certainly put the shares under additional pressure despite B2Gold successfully reaching an agreement.

It seems by announcing the existing CFO as a replacement, the board wanted continuity from someone who knows the business rather than some outsider. You'd hope that a former CFO would also bring increased capital discipline to the business and improved capital allocation. At the current share price trading well below peer multiples, you'd hope there would be a focus on buybacks in the future.

My main concern with appointing a CFO is whether there is less of a focus on operational performance and more on the financial side. One hopes that the new CEO is willing to listen to the wider management team, who are more experienced on the operational side of the business, while balancing this with good capital allocation.

There remains some uncertainty at this stage as to the scale of the changes, if any, a new CEO will bring. But one can only hope they allow B2Gold's performance to improve to no longer lag peers.

Valuation

With the gold price reaching a record high this year, B2Gold's share price, like many of its peers, had a strong rally, before its recent weakness. With all gold miners' earnings reliant on the price of gold, a peer valuation is appropriate to get a relative valuation. With all miners having large differences in cash and debt levels, I take account of this by opting for an enterprise value to EBITDA multiple comparison.

I opted to compare to peers Gold Fields (GFI), AngloGold Ashanti (AU), Equinox Gold (EQX), Barrick Mining (B), Agnico Eagle (AEM), and IAMGOLD (IAG). These were chosen as they are all in-production miners; some have overlap with B2Gold in terms of jurisdictions of operation, and they provide varying sizes of miners to compare to.

B2Gold currently trades at an EV to EBITDA multiple of 3.076, the lowest multiple compared to its peers. This, I believe, is unjustified. B2Gold has successfully opened its Goose mine in Canada, a tier-one jurisdiction, and now operates across four jurisdictions. Although B2Gold warrants a significant discount due to its significant exposure to Mali and will never trade at the levels of a pure tier-one jurisdictional miner like Agnico Eagle, this discount does appear excessive, with most peers trading at a multiple around 5.67% higher than B2Gold's current multiple.

To determine a price value for B2Gold, I will calculate the free cash flow and apply a multiple to this. Given the 264,768 ounces of gold prepayment due between July 2025 and June 2026, I will take what I believe to be the target level production for 2027.

I assume annualized production from Q4 '26 onwards of 1.17 million ounces, made up of 300,000 ounces from Goose, Masbate producing 180,000 ounces, 80,000 ounces from Otjikoto, and 610,000 ounces from Fekola, made up of 180,000 ounces from Fekola Regional and 440,000 ounces from the existing Fekola Mine.

At a gold price of $5,000/oz, I forecast an AISC for Q4 '26 onwards of $1,814/oz. This was calculated by using the 2026 guidance for Otjikoto and Masbate, given production is set to remain stable during the period, and a forecast increase on Goose's life of mine plan given increased royalty costs, and assuming Fekola AISC falls back to 2025 levels as higher production comes online with reduced stripping, again adjusted higher due to increased royalty costs.

I adjust the AISC by $12 for every $100 change in the gold price, the same sensitivity factor used by B2Gold in their 2026 guidance. I assume a 30% tax rate and $0 million in interest costs. As of Q4 2025, B2Gold had $380 million in cash and a total debt of $598 million. Given B2Gold's free cash flow generation, I expect this net debt to become a net-cash position within the next 12 months, justifying a zero-interest cost assumption.

Using a gold price of $3,600 an ounce, 30% below the current gold price to give a margin of safety, I anticipate cash flow per share of $1.20. I apply a cash flow multiplier of x6, just below the average of the mining sector, taking into account that following commercial production at Goose, B2Gold is a more diversified miner but also accounting for the higher risks that come from the Mali operations. This gives a target share price of $7.20 per share, 42% higher than the current share price.

I continue to believe that B2Gold remains a buy at this price, though I am downgrading from a strong buy following the recent rally. When I last gave a Strong Buy rating in November, the share price was $4.10, with a similar forecast target price estimate; they now trade at $5.10, leaving less upside.

The recent rise in the oil price potentially puts more upward pressure on operating costs given mining's energy-intensive nature, and jurisdictional risk from Mali will continue to weigh on valuation.

That said, with Goose now in production, a strong balance sheet, and other expansion projects in the works, I am happy to hold B2Gold's shares and believe they remain a buy.

The biggest risk for B2Gold is the same as any other gold miner: the price of gold and its corresponding impact on the revenue and earnings of B2Gold, which greatly influence the share price.

A further important risk is the jurisdictional risk B2Gold faces. The large exposure to Mali, a high-risk mining jurisdiction with a history of taking control of mines and the arrest of gold mining executives, means B2Gold may face sudden changes in terms of regulations and uncertainty. Despite this, B2Gold does appear to have a good working relationship with Mali's government, reaching an agreement with regard to the new mining codes and the recent granting of the permit for Fekola underground. Whilst this seems positive, with Mali having seized other mines, investors hearing that B2Gold has operations in Mali will always weigh on the valuation.

Conclusion

B2Gold exited 2025 with momentum, with both production and revenues at record highs as the Goose mine reached commercial production.

2026 guidance on the surface appeared weak, but there has to be an understanding that this will be a transition year. It's all about setting up for a stronger 2027 and reflective of higher royalties from the current gold price. With the gold prepay agreement expiring in June 2026 this offers another boost. This allows 66,000 ounces of quarterly production to be sold at market prices, currently ~$5,100/oz, compared to the $2,191/oz in the prepay, suggesting a significant uplift to revenue could be coming.

At current prices, B2Gold trades at a valuation well below its peers, a valuation that looks increasingly unjustified as Goose ramps up. The balance sheet is strong, production is growing, and once the gold prepay completes this year, free cash flow will hit the accelerator.

While Mali will continue to weigh on valuation and the rising oil price may increase costs, the combination of B2Gold setting itself up to grow production makes the recent pullback look like a potential opportunity. For this reason, I consider the shares to have a "buy" rating.
